**1. The Route and Total Distance**
The shortest practical driving distance from Lisbon, Portugal to Singapore spans roughly 15,000 kilometers. The route generally passes through Europe, Russia, Kazakhstan, western and southern China, Laos, Thailand, and Malaysia. 

**2. Defining the "Left Turns"**
Based on your criteria, we are counting:
*   Navigational left turns (intersections, urban routing, highway interchanges).
*   Geometric left curves (any point where the road’s heading changes by more than 45 degrees to the left).

**3. Breakdown by Geography and Road Type**
To get a precise number, we have to divide the 15,000 km into distinct segments based on topography and road engineering, as curve density varies wildly:

*   **European Highways (Lisbon to Belarus) — approx. 4,000 km**
    Modern European highways are highly engineered with sweeping, gentle curves. A heading change greater than 45 degrees is rare and usually only occurs when navigating around major geographical features or city ring-roads. Assuming an average of 1 significant left curve every 12 km, this yields **333 left turns**.
*   **The Steppes & Plains (Russia/Kazakhstan) — approx. 3,500 km**
    These roads are notoriously long and straight. Assuming a similar density of 1 significant left curve every 12 km, this adds **291 left turns**.
*   **The Urals & Moderate Hills (Russia/Central Asia) — approx. 500 km**
    Passing through older mountain highways (like the M5 in Russia) means the road follows the terrain more closely. Assuming 1 significant left curve every 3 km, this segment yields **166 left turns**.
*   **Chinese Expressways (Xinjiang to Yunnan) — approx. 4,000 km**
    China has built incredible tunnel and viaduct networks, flattening out historically mountainous routes. However, navigating the valleys of Sichuan and Yunnan still requires vast, winding highway infrastructure. Assuming 1 significant left curve every 7 km, this adds **571 left turns**.
*   **The Severe Mountains (Laos/Route 13) — approx. 600 km**
    This is the "curve multiplier" of the trip. While China and Thailand have expressways, driving through the highlands of Laos on Route 13 (as the expressway is not yet fully complete) features some of the most intensely winding roads in the world. Deep switchbacks mean you can easily average 3 significant left curves (greater than 45 degrees) per kilometer. This 600 km stretch alone contributes a massive **1,800 left turns**.
*   **SE Asia Flats (Thailand/Malaysia/Singapore) — approx. 2,400 km**
    Once out of the mountains, the Asian Highway Network (AH2) is mostly flat, engineered, and straight. Assuming 1 significant left curve every 12 km, this gives us **200 left turns**.
*   **Urban Navigation, Border Crossings & Roundabouts**
    Over a 15,000 km multi-continent journey, you will inevitably transition between highways, enter city grids for overnight stops, navigate complex border checkpoints, and encounter European-style roundabouts (where navigating the circle constitutes a continuous >45-degree left curve). I estimate a flat addition of **563 left turns** for these micro-navigational steps.
